nuggets.headline: Daft Punk's signature 'Homework' groove wasn't programmed in a modern DAW, but meticulously punched into a humble 8-track MIDI sequencer from the 1980s.
nuggets.text: The entire rhythmic and melodic framework for *Homework* was sequenced on an Alesis MMT-8. This hardware sequencer, known for its pattern-based workflow, forced a loop-centric approach and imparted a specific timing feel that's distinct from the sample-accurate grid of today's software. This limitation became a core part of their sound, creating a raw, machine-like funk that was then heavily processed through their Mackie mixer and analog effects.

source.title: Unearthed '90s magazine clipping reveals Daft Punk's ...
source.quoteSnippet: The duo were running an Apple Macintosh with EMAGIC MicroLogic, as well as an Alesis MMT-8 for all their sequencing needs...

nuggets.imageCaption: The Alesis MMT-8, the hardware sequencer at the heart of Homework's groove.

nuggets.headline: The hypnotic pulse of "Around the World" isn't just the beat; it's a single, five-note bassline doing all the harmonic work through masterful filter modulation.
nuggets.text: The track's entire harmonic structure rests on a deceptively simple bass ostinato. There are no traditional chord changes; instead, Daft Punk creates dynamics and tension by manipulating the cutoff frequency of a low-pass filter on the bass and synth layers. Listen closely around 1:15, where a subtle filter sweep opens up, adding brightness and energy to the groove without ever changing the fundamental loop, a hallmark technique of the French house sound.

source.title: Daft Punk's Homework turns 25: discover the gear and ...
source.quoteSnippet: The use of filter sweeps on the bass and synth elements creates a sense of movement and variation within the repetitive loop, which is a hallmark of French house.

nuggets.imageCaption: A diagram showing how a low-pass filter shapes a synthesizer's sound.

nuggets.headline: If you think the original is hypnotic, you have to hear how French Touch pioneers Motorbass completely deconstructed and rewired "Around The World" for their official remixes.
nuggets.text: In 1997, fellow Parisians Motorbass (Philippe Zdar and Boom Bass) delivered the 'Miami Mix' and 'Vice Mix' of the track. These aren't simple dance remixes; they are a parallel-universe version from within the same scene, stripping the original down and rebuilding it with a grittier, more menacing low-end. Hearing Zdar's take is like listening to a direct dialogue between the architects of the French Touch movement.

source.title: Deeper Than Daft Punk:. Rewinding the disco-driven filter ...
source.quoteSnippet: Motorbass even remixed 'Around The World,' which makes perfect sense as they were the godfathers of this sound.

nuggets.imageCaption: Motorbass, the French duo who delivered a legendary remix of the track.

artistSummary: Daft Punk's sonic fingerprint is a product of a fiercely DIY ethos, built on the limitations and character of vintage hardware. Their mastery lies in transforming simple, repetitive loops into hypnotic grooves through meticulous sequencing, aggressive compression, and the tactile manipulation of analog filters, defining the sound of 90s French house.
